data.title: The Best Risk-Adjusted Way To Bet On Energy Right Now
data.description:
The energy sector (XLE) is pretty sophisticated, with numerous ways to access it, both upstream, refineries (DK)(MPC), and midstream infrastructure businesses, that respond differently to supply-demand shifts, policy, geopolitical events, and capital spending cycles. At the moment, I believe that midstream infrastructure (MLPA) is a far more attractive way to invest in the space than in traditional upstream opportunities. While I do own some from both categories, this time I'm going to detail why midstream is a better opportunity right now.

The Structural Headwinds Facing Oil Producers And Refiners

According to the International Energy Agency, global demand for oil (USO), coal, and natural gas (UNG) is expected to peak over the next several years, no later than 2030. While it is likely that energy demand for these fossil fuels will remain resilient for years to come, future oil demand growth beyond that level after 2030 is likely to remain flat and even slightly decline from a little over 100 million barrels per day to around 93 million barrels per day.

Meanwhile, there are several dynamics that could push oil supply up, which could push oil prices down materially if there becomes a multi-million barrel per day surplus in the coming years. This could come from several places. One, Permian production increases as efficiencies continue to improve. Two, an increase in Venezuelan oil production in the coming years, assuming the United States’ efforts to control that industry succeed, and leverage as a way of economic warfare against Russia and potentially Iran.

Additionally, the U.S. EIA projects U.S. gasoline prices to decline by nearly 20 cents per gallon year over year this year, which will likely weigh on refining margins. Moreover, if crude prices fall, as the EIA projects, it could also hurt upstream producer profits.

Meanwhile, even though the current Trump administration is pushing against the greening of the energy supply chain in favor of renewables (ICLN), efforts in other countries are underway to continue pursuing renewable alternatives. For example, China is aggressively ramping up solar and hydro power generation, as well as nuclear power.

Europe is pursuing a similar path, and eventually the Democrats will likely regain power in the United States, which will likely cause a fresh tailwind for renewable energy in the country as well.

Additionally, electric vehicles are growing dramatically, with forecasts for about 10 times more electric vehicles globally by 2030. Estimates are that about 50% of new U.S. car registrations will be electric by 2030. While that forecast was made in late 2023, while President Biden was still in office and the outcome of the 2024 election was still uncertain, even if that target is pushed back materially, the trend will still likely be toward more electric vehicles over time.

Why Midstream Cash Flows Are Far More Resilient

This all points to the uncertain outlook for energy producers and refiners. Certainly, geopolitical events can emerge, which can cause oil prices to spike, and energy producers will likely mint a lot of cash in that type of environment, especially if they can continue to cut production costs. However, that creates a much less certain profile for returns, which inherently makes them much more speculative investments and therefore harder to value.

Certainly, some companies have proven their resilience over time, like ExxonMobil (XOM), Chevron (CVX), and Canadian Natural Resources (CNQ), as they have been able to grow their dividends consistently year after year over the course of many decades, even in the face of volatile energy prices. Therefore, as long as an attractive valuation can be achieved on those stocks, especially in terms of their dividend yield, when purchasing them, I think investors can do pretty well.

That's because those management teams have proven to be very prudent allocators of capital and do not overcommit on their dividends. Therefore, if a dividend yield is at a high enough level, there's a good chance that they will be able to continue not only paying that dividend, but even increasing it in the years to come, thereby making that a good baseline valuation metric for those companies. However, absent such a bargain, I think midstream infrastructure is a much better buy.

The reason for this is that they are much less commodity price sensitive, as the vast majority of their cash flows come from fee-based contracted cash flows, even in cases where their earnings are volume-determined. Demand is fairly inelastic, so their volumes do not tend to vary too wildly, even with significant swings in commodity prices. Additionally, many of these companies have minimum volume commitments to lock in a certain degree of profitability on their assets.

On top of that, they tend to have inflation-linked tariffs on many of their infrastructure assets, which makes them good long-term cash flow machines that can underpin a dividend growth investment strategy. In fact, many midstream companies have very high yields and grow those distributions and dividends at inflation-beating clips year after year.

Examples of this include Enbridge (ENB), Williams Companies (WMB), TC Energy (TRP), Enterprise Products Partners (EPD), MPLX (MPLX), and several others, with the likes of ENB and EPD each growing their dividends for over a quarter-century. The yields in this sector are extremely attractive, too, as evidenced by the fact that the Alerian MLP ETF (AMLP) currently offers an over 8% yield, which is nearly double what is offered by leading energy sector ETFs.

Investor Takeaway

While I am far from bearish on energy prices, as I think the markets have not yet fully come to grips with the inflation and debasement risks facing the U.S. dollar, as well as the significant geopolitical risks that could at any moment inflate the price of oil significantly, not to mention the AI boom, which would be a strong tailwind for natural gas prices over time, I still favor midstream today.

This is because midstream cash flows are much more resilient, thereby making them much easier to value. As long as you can buy them at an attractive discount, I have much higher confidence in their ability to deliver a satisfactory return over time than energy companies, even if in a bull market scenario for oil, energy producers will likely significantly outperform midstream.

Additionally, their high and tax-advantaged yields, especially from K-1 MLPs in a taxable account, make for attractive income and compounding machines for investors who want to allocate capital in a taxable account. As a result, midstream is now the largest sector in my 7–8% yielding, low-beta, total return outperforming portfolios at High Yield Investor.

data.title: Corvus Pharmaceuticals: ITK Inhibitor Soquelitinib AD Data Could Be Improved Upon
data.description:
The last time I spoke about Corvus Pharmaceuticals, Inc. (CRVS), it was in a Seeking Alpha article entitled "Corvus Pharmaceuticals: Positive Phase 1 Soquelitinib Trial Data Brings 2025 Catalysts." With respect to this article, I noted that the company had obtained some positive results from its ongoing phase 1 study using its ITK inhibitor soquelitinib to treat patients with moderate-to-severe atopic dermatitis [AD]. I also mentioned that the company was set to release results from cohort 4 of this early-stage study as well.

I'm happy to report that the company was indeed able to release highly positive data from this specific cohort of the trial. More about this below, but it was found that about 75% of patients who received 200 mg of soquelitinib twice daily achieved EASI 75. The point is that longer treatment duration resulted in an improved clinical outcome for these patients, and that such an effect persisted even one month after treatment was stopped.

Plus, it was also able to help patients who had received prior therapies as well. It is not guaranteed, but possible that this drug could become the leading oral therapy of choice to treat these patients with AD. I'm also happy to note that since I wrote my prior Seeking Alpha article [highlighted above at the beginning], the stock has gained 339% since then. It traded higher by 66% just the other day in pre-market on the back of the release of positive data from the phase 1 AD study. If gains have happened, then why do I believe there is more value to be left here? That's because, first of all, soquelitinib is not a "one-hit wonder." It is already being evaluated in an ongoing phase 3 study to treat adult patients with relapsed/refractory peripheral T-cell lymphoma [PTCL].

In addition, it is being explored in an ongoing phase 2 study in collaboration with the National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases [NIAID] to treat patients with Autoimmune Lymphoproliferative Syndrome [ALPS]. Secondly, there is a catalyst expected to happen soon, which is that the company intends to initiate a phase 2 study using soquelitinib to treat patients with moderate-to-severe AD who had failed at least one prior topical or systemic therapy in Q1 of 2026.

With extremely positive durable data from the phase 1 study using soquelitinib to treat AD patients, plus the ability to expand its pipeline full of ITK inhibitors, I believe that this company's stock should remain as a Strong Buy rating. The beauty is that ITK inhibition can be used to go after both immune-mediated disorders and cancers. Thus, the point is that there is expansive potential to go after many other indications with soquelitinib. Plus, its next-generation ITK inhibitors are also working on as well.

Cohort 4 Surprise Data Bodes Well for Shareholder Value and Next Step

As I noted above, Corvus Pharmaceuticals was able to report positive results from its phase 1 study using its ITK inhibitor soquelitinib for the treatment of patients with moderate-to-severe atopic dermatitis [AD]. I'm going to go over in detail below why the data from this study is impressive. Not just the scope of being positive, but creating a competitive edge over other already approved therapies for these AD patients. Before going over the release of the positive data from this early-stage trial, plus any catalysts that are to come from this program, I believe that it is first important to go over what this disorder is and what the possible market opportunity could end up being.

Atopic Dermatitis [AD] is characterized as a chronic inflammatory skin disorder where the skin becomes dry, itchy, and inflamed. Although it can occur in literally any person, it tends to be more common in children. It can be irritating because of the itching, which is a very bad thing; on the flip side, it is not at all contagious. Besides itching, there are several other symptoms that can flare up for these patients, and they are as follows:

  • Cracked skin
  • Oozing from skin
  • Small raised bumps
  • Rash.

Another important factor is to state that AD itself can actually branch out to asthma and allergies. In terms of asthma, the compromised skin barrier could be the cause of this. The AD itself can also lead to specific food allergies for a patient as well. The global atopic dermatitis market, in the 7 major markets [7MM], is expected to reach $22.4 billion by 2033. This is a very large market, and the company with its oral ITK inhibitor soquelitinib might be able to conquer the entire pool of patients. It is believed that it could be the most active new oral medicine to treat 1st-line or later AD patients. This all sounds good, but how the heck does ITK inhibition work to treat AD patients and other inflammatory disorders? It goes after multiple T-cell differentiations [Corvus Phase 1 Atopic Dermatitis Data January 2026, slide #5], and this can be illustrated as follows:

  • Th1 is activated - This involves the elimination of cancer cells and viruses.
  • Th2 is blocked - This allows blocking of cytokines IL-4, IL-5, and IL-13 that drive the inflammatory response.
  • Th17 is blocked - This allows blocking of cytokines IL-17, IL-21, IL-22, and IL-23 that drive inflammation response.

Th2 and Th17 are involved in autoimmune, inflammatory, fibrotic, and allergic diseases. The blocking of ITK also allows for a change in regulatory T-cells [Tregs]. There is the formation of T-regs, which allows for a greater decrease of the amount of Th17.

In order to determine if the company would be able to be effective in using soquelitinib to treat patients with moderate-to-severe AD, it randomized patients 1:1 in a phase 1 study for cohort 4 as follows:

  • 12 patients who received 200 mg of soquelitinib twice daily.
  • 10 patients who received placebo [2 patients were removed because they were not compliant because of not being evaluable at Day 56].

The point here with cohort 4 is that it was to re-establish safety/efficacy from cohorts 1-3 but to see if it could be improved upon with a longer duration study. The main clinical endpoint being looked at is something known as the Eczema Area and Severity Index [EASI], which looks at 4 anatomical regions of a person's body. There is a score given for these patients from "0" to "72," with the higher number indicating greater disease severity. To that end, the EASI score is to look at improvement from baseline. With EASI 75, it means the percentage of patients that achieve a greater than or equal to 75% improvement from baseline. Thus, in this study, it was noted that 75% of the patients given 200 mg of soquelitinib twice daily achieved EASI 75.

I brought up this EASI number because this is what improved the most in cohort 4 compared to that of cohorts 1-3. The thing is that there is a catalyst that is to happen soon, any day now. That is, the company is expected to initiate a phase 2 study using this drug to treat patients with moderate-to-severe AD in Q1 of 2026. I believe it is important to keep a Strong Buy rating on this company's stock on this progress alone. But I believe there is room for improvement here. How so? Well, the company is designing this study to not only test out 200 mg soquelitinib twice daily, but it is looking at two other dosing options as well:

  • 200 mg soquelitinib once daily.
  • 400 mg soquelitinib once daily.

Plus, another bullish aspect of the design is that it will be a longer study at 12 weeks, with a 30-day follow-up period of no treatment. The point is that it is possible for the EASI 75 number I listed above to be improved upon even more. Lastly, this drug appears to help patients who are treatment resistant. One drug used to treat these patients with AD is DUPIXENT [dupilumab] from Sanofi (SNY)/Regeneron Pharmaceuticals (REGN) for starters. Then, there is another drug known as RINVOQ [upadacitinib] from AbbVie (ABBV). I bring these drug candidates up because patients who became treatment resistant to them ended up responding to ITK inhibitor soquelitinib [Corvus Phase 1 Atopic Dermatitis Data January 2026, slide #25].

The next thing now is for the company to move towards phase 2 testing, but it did indeed establish that ITK inhibition works. Thus, not only can it move soquelitinib forward, but it will be able to advance its next-generation ITK inhibitors as well.

Financials

According to the 10-Q SEC Filing, Corvus Pharmaceuticals had cash, cash equivalents, and marketable securities of $65.7 million as of September 30, 2025. The truth is that it didn't have a lot of cash on hand, and the reason why is because of the cash runway projection it laid out. It is believed that this will only be enough cash on hand to fund its operations at least into Q4 of 2026. With that being said, it enacted an underwritten public offering of common stock to raise approximately $150 million. There is a chance to raise a bit more cash as well, because it is intended for the company to grant the underwriters a 30-day option to purchase an additional $22.5 million worth of shares of common stock at the very same price.

The company burns a total of $10.5 million of cash per quarter. This is broken down into $8.45 million in R&D expenses and then $2.11 million in G&A expenses.

Risks to Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in Corvus Pharmaceuticals. The first risk to consider would be in terms of the evaluation of soquelitinib in the soon-to-be-initiated phase 2 study targeting patients with moderate-to-severe AD.

The risk here is that even though Cohort 4 saw a huge increase of 75% of patients achieving EASI 75, there is no assurance that this is going to be replicated in the next study. In addition, the hope is that a higher dose of 400 mg of soquelitinib or a longer duration study of 12 weeks could improve upon the efficacy established thus far. There is no guarantee that changing these features of the trial is going to allow the company to achieve an even greater outcome than what has been observed thus far.

The second risk to consider would be in terms of the ongoing phase 3 study using the ITK inhibitor soquelitinib for the treatment of patients with relapsed/refractory peripheral T-cell lymphoma [PTCL]. The risk here is that there is no assurance that the company's drug is going to do better than physicians' choice of either belinostat or pralatrexate. Nor that the primary endpoint of progression-free survival [PFS] is going to be met in a statistically significant manner.

The third and final risk to consider would be the next-generation ITK inhibitors it is working on. In the pipeline, there are two undisclosed next-generation ITK inhibitors known as ITKi #1 and ITKi #2 being developed to treat immune disorders.

The risk here is that even though Corvus Pharmaceuticals has been successful in using soquelitinib to treat patients with asthma, there is no guarantee that these next-generation candidates will perform as well or better in treating this or any other immune-mediated disorder. Nor that the company is going to be able to advance either of these candidates past preclinical testing and eventually evaluate them in phase 1 clinical studies.

Conclusion

Corvus Pharmaceuticals had some amazing data, especially as I have shown above, where it marked an improvement of EASI 75 from cohorts 1 to 3 compared to that of cohort 4 from the phase 1 study. Soquelitinib appears to do better when given over a long period of time, but at the same time without sacrificing safety. The beauty of this company is that it is well equipped to move on towards phase 2 clinical testing now. If it can either show a higher dose of 400 mg of soquelitinib to do well or a longer duration of 12 weeks to achieve a superior outcome, then further gains can potentially be made here.

With positive data from the phase 1 study using soquelitinib to treat moderate-to-severe AD patients who failed at least one prior topical or systemic therapy, plus the expected initiation of the phase 2 study for this very same program in Q1 of 2026, I believe that this company's stock should remain with a Strong Buy rating.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Navitas 2.0: GaN And SiC Powerhouse Pivoting Into The AI Data Center Boom
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Navitas Semiconductor (NVTS) is well positioned and right at the center of AI's exploding power needs, especially the global drive for energy efficiency. I am rating the company a Strong Buy. My rating stems from the company's decisive pivot away from commoditized mobile chargers towards serving high-power, high-margin markets like AI data centers, cloud computing, EV infrastructure, and industrial electrification. Navitas has a proven GaN technology and a newly added SiC portfolio. Under the CEO, Chris Allexandre, NVTS has not wasted any time focusing resources on these big opportunities;

...We will accelerate, pivot and double down on those high power markets and customers as we move away from consumer and mobile. I call this Navitas 2.0...

My point is that Navitas GaN, together with the SiC technology platform, gives it a critical edge as the industry shifts to higher voltages and greater efficiency. GaN is already what I would call "mainstream for AI data centers, performance computing, and industrial electrification." And the recent "NVIDIA 800V DC AI factory ecosystem announcement is the first proof point" of GaN adoption in cutting-edge server power architecture. Meanwhile, NVTS's GeneSiC division provides high-voltage SiC chips targeting energy storage, solar, EV charging, and grid applications. And I would say that there are very few, if any, competitors that can offer this comprehensive GaN + SiC solution. This means that Navitas can address power needs from the server rack all the way to the electrical substation.

Another reason why I am rating the company a Strong Buy is NVTS' engagement with top-tier customers. In his first 60 days, the new CEO met a wide range of hyperscalers, AI hardware firms, and industrial players

... every single customer... gave me the same... GaN and high voltage SiC technologies are the solution... They view Navitas at the center of this transformation...

I believe this implies that NVTS is already part of NVIDIA's partner ecosystem for 800V DV data centers and is aligning its go-to-market directly with hyperscalers, GPU/CPU OEMs, and tier 1 power supply vendors. Ideally, to me, this means that Navitas is plugged into the AI revolution's powertrain. As AI data centers transition to new power architectures [48V, 400V, 800V DC] to support skyrocketing electricity needs, NVTS chips are positioned to be design-in favorites. The same goes for the green energy side. The shift to EV fast charging, grid battery storage, and renewable infrastructure requires higher voltage semiconductors—exactly Navitas's domain.

One important factor that I need to highlight is that Navitas has the balance sheet to execute this opportunity. The company has over $150Mn in cash and $0 debt. And as of now, the company can sustain its R&D and customer engagements through the current revenue. Management is also cutting costs and reallocating spending toward high-power programs. In my view, the market underappreciates how deliberately NVTS is trading short-term pain for long-term gain. The Q4 2025 guidance on revenue is a dip that is largely self-inflicted to reset the business, clear excess channel inventory, and exit low-value markets. I say this because it is a partly deliberate decision to walk away from revenue they didn't like anymore while also cleaning up the pipes in the channel so they can restart growth from a cleaner base. On the earnings call, the CFO, Todd Glickman, said that Q4 revenue reduction reflects a "strategic decision to deprioritize" the low-power, lower-profit China mobile business. Therefore, as a result, I expect 2026 to show a much cleaner growth story that is driven by AI, data centers, and industrial wins.

Business Overview

So, what does Navitas actually do? Navitas is a fabless semiconductor company specializing in next-gen power electronics. It was founded in 2014 and was a pioneer in commercializing GaN power ICs. These are ultra-efficient chips that switch faster than traditional silicon, enabling smaller and more energy-saving power converters. Navitas rode the GaN smartphone charger wave, shipping hundreds of millions of units and establishing itself as a leader in GaN reliability and performance. However, the company always had its sights on bigger arenas beyond mobile. I would say that as of now, Navitas is no longer a GaN phone charger company. It is now positioning itself as a broad-based power semiconductor innovator. The company's R&D is centered on enabling the next generation of power systems across multiple industries.

Growth Drivers

First is AI data center electrification. The proliferation of AI supercomputing is straining the limits of conventional data center power designs. Companies like Meta (META) and Microsoft (MSFT) are reportedly rethinking power delivery, moving to higher voltage DC distribution to improve efficiency. Nvidia's reference AI factory design uses 800V DC bus architecture, and NVTS is a key partner supplying GaN and SiC components into this ecosystem. This is definitely a huge vote of confidence in Navitas tech. And while revenue from these AI data center wins is just beginning, it expects meaningful ramp-up by 2026 and 2027 as 800V architectures go mainstream. In the meantime, NVTS is working with multiple hyperscalers and server OEMs to design its GaN Fast chips into next-gen power supplies. Even standard 48V server power designs can benefit from GaN efficiency, so NVTS should see growing GaN revenue in data centers by 2026, even ahead of the big 800V transition. At the end of the day, AI is a powerful catalyst, as put by the CEO;

...AI is really a catalyst that drives change... AI data centers... require a lot more power. We see an acceleration of that demand to higher power...

The second driver that I see is the ramping adoption of GaN and SiC. Beyond AI, a broad secular shift to GaN/SiC is underway in the power electronics industry. And this is why they are displacing silicon in everything from laptop chargers to electric vehicles. Navitas is at the forefront of this shift. Its latest 100V GaN FETs target the fast-growing market for high-power server point-of-load converters and gaming PC supplies. Meanwhile, in the EV and renewable space, Navitas's SiC MOSFETs and diodes are gaining traction. For example, NVTS won a design with Enphase for solar microinverters and is pursuing EV inverter opportunities. The next 1 to 2 years will see new products launched from NVTS that fill out its lineup. And as a result, I expect to see higher current GaN ICs, perhaps GaN ICs with integrated drivers for servers, and continued expansion of the SiC module portfolio. Yet each new product opens doors to additional customers.

Valuation

By traditional metrics, NVTS stock looks expensive. The company has very low current revenue and is not yet profitable. Yet its market cap is around $2.5B. This yields eye-popping valuation ratios. For instance, FWD Price/sales is at 55.22x versus a 3.57x sector median. NVTS's EV of $2.51Bn implies about 52.06x EV/Sales on 2025 revenues.

In comparison with other peers, they trade at far lower multiples. For instance, Wolfspeed (WOLF), a leading pure-play SiC Company, trades at a fraction of Navitas's sales multiple. WOLF's P/S is about 7x, albeit with its own issues. Larger, diversified players like ON Semiconductor (ON) or Infineon (IFNNY) command FWD P/S ratios in the mid-single digits and P/E multiples under 30x. ON, for instance, has its FWD P/S at 4.04x while its FWD Non-GAAP P/E is at 25.75x. On the other side, IFNNY has its FWD P/S at 3.48x while its FWD Non-GAAP P/E is at 26.75x.

Nevertheless, I would argue that these plain vanilla multiples are misleading at this stage. NVTS is in a transformational period. It is at a revenue low point and investing heavily for future growth. If I fast forward to 2027, NVTS's financial profile could be entirely different. I expect potentially a few hundred million in annual revenue, gross margins trending towards 50%, and a path to profitability. In this regard, the current market cap might end up looking quite reasonable. Rather than relying on PEG, which may not be meaningful given the negative earnings, I would say that the valuation of NVTS is best reconciled through the lens of anticipated growth and operating leverage. This is given the future growth prospects that the company has highlighted. I believe that if management executes and achieves 50%+ annual revenue growth for several years, the PEG will drop and more traditional P/E metrics will come into view.

Navitas' Key Risk

The biggest risk I see for NVTS is customer concentration and Hyperscaler ramp. And this is because it leans on a few large customers in the AI & data center space, driving future revenue. This concentration means execution on those customers' programs is critical. If, for instance, NVDA's 800V AI factory rollout is slower than expected or a hyperscaler delays deployment, NVTS's revenue ramp could lag.

There is also an execution risk in transformation. NVTS is effectively rebuilding its airplane midflight. Shifting its target market, product focus, and organizational structure. And all this is done while still burning cash. Such a major pivot carries execution risk. Delays in new product development, difficulty in breaking into entrenched competitor accounts, or simply overextending a small team. The company is also undertaking cost cuts and workforce realignment; morale and focus need to stay high through these changes.

Final Thoughts

At the end of the day, the company's unique dual focus on GaN and SiC sets it apart from the crowd. It is one of the only pure plays that can address the full spectrum of next-gen power needs. And under the new CEO, NVTS has a clear strategic vision. By 2027, I believe NVTS will be recognized as a key enabler in AI infrastructure and green energy with revenues to match. And that's why I am initiating coverage on Navitas Semiconductors with a Strong Buy rating.

data.title: Roblox: Growth Story The Market Forgot
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Roblox Corporation (RBLX) is a rapidly growing business. However, you wouldn't necessarily remember this given the malaise in its share price.

Now, what complicates matters is that there are some clear tangible reasons for why the stock finds itself in this situation, for example, its clear and undeniable use of stock-based compensation, or SBC, being one of the main reasons, not to mention the fact that its growth rates are somewhat challenging to predict.

Consequently, there's a lot to be critical of here. But at a 40% discount from where the stock was last summer, it's clear that a lot of pesky considerations have already been priced in.

In sum, I like RBLX, and may take a position in this stock.

Roblox's Near-Term Prospects

Roblox is a business that I've been bullish on in the past, about a year ago. Read here for free.

Indeed, Roblox doesn't need much introduction. As you know, it's a huge online platform where people don’t just play games but can make them, too.

Further, millions of creators build experiences while players jump between all different types of games.

Next, its value proposition is that anyone can create something and if a game takes off, creators can make serious money from it. Roblox wants engagement and gives developers the tools and the gamers.

Furthermore, the business has strong momentum, as daily usage remains very strong, with older players showing up in bigger numbers too.

With this background in mind, let's discuss its fundamentals.

Unpredictable Revenue Growth Rates For 2026

I alluded to this in the introduction. The one thing that investors hate is not knowing what sort of growth rates are sustainable. Clearly, as Roblox guides for more than 40% y/y revenue growth rates in its Q4 2025 earnings report: the business is on fire.

However, the question is never about the next quarter. The question is, ''...and what's next''? What sort of growth rates can this business organically deliver in 2026–2027?

To further illustrate my point, consider that just 2 quarters ago, the business had delivered 21% y/y revenue growth rates. And now, Q4 2025 is expected to deliver as much as 42% y/y revenue growth rates.

This naturally begs the question, what sort of outlook is reasonable for 2026? For a stock to sustain a premium, investors crave predictability, not just growth for growth's sake, but growth plus predictability plus sustainability.

Given this context, let's now discuss RBLX's premium on its stock.

RBLX Stock Valuation - 39x Free Cash Flow

Before we get stuck into its valuation, as an Inflection investor, I believe that a business with a strong balance sheet can meaningfully contribute to the overall investment thesis.

Consequently, Roblox has approximately $4 billion of net cash, which is not only in a very strong position, but this high net cash position means that more than 7% of its market cap is made up of cash. Hence, this strong financial position is a significant contributor to the bull case.

And this brings me to the key controversial argument: RBLX's premium on its stock. Allow me to explain the main issue overhanging RBLX.

Last Q4 2024, Roblox delivered $121 million of free cash flow. And you'd assume that in Q4 2025, given its meaningfully larger scale, that its free cash flow would be up at least 50% y/y. After all, in Q2 2025, its free cash flow was up 58% y/y, followed by its free cash flow being up 103% y/y in Q3 2025.

However, the follow-through of its strong free cash flow doesn't appear to be happening. In fact, RBLX's guidance for Q4 points to its free cash flow coming in at the high-end around $130 million.

Even if management is being conservative and its free cash flow reaches $145 million, this would only be a 20% jump in free cash flow relative to the same period a year ago.

Naturally, this generates all sorts of questions. How can the business be growing at such a rapid rate, and its free cash flow not increase at a strong and consistent rate?

Another crucial question is, why is management on pace for more than $1.4 billion of stock-based compensation in 2026, if investors are not seeing its free cash flow percolate to shareholders?

Yet another question is this: in Q3 2025, total diluted shares were up 7% y/y. Now that its share price is down 40% from its highs, does management team more stock-based compensation to be correctly incentivized?

On the other hand, assuming that the business continues to deliver roughly 25% free cash flow margins, Roblox could report about $1.6 billion of free cash flow.

So, this is my question: given all these pesky but pertinent questions, does paying 39x forward free cash flow make sense? I believe it's certainly a fair multiple for Roblox.

Yes, there are a lot of uncertainties and unpleasant questions left unanswered. But I believe that those are by now mostly priced in now at a $55 billion market cap.

The Bottom Line

In short, there are issues here, such its heavy use of stock-based comp, plus its free cash flow growth hasn’t been perfectly smooth. On top of that, the visibility into 2026 isn’t crystal clear.

But let's take a step back and think this through, it’s hard to ignore what Roblox is doing achieving: very strong top-line momentum, while massively increasing its free cash flow over time.

Additionally, more than 7% of its market cap is made up of net cash.

Thus, at today’s price, a lot of the bad news is now baked in, while the upside from sustained free cash flow isn't.

All in all, paying 39x forward free cash flow for a business of this quality feels reasonable, and I may consider a position myself.

data.title: 4 Potential Gray Swans That Keep Me Up At Night
data.description:
Markets had a bit of a "hiccup" in trading on Tuesday. Growing tensions around Greenland and the potential for new tariffs sent both the NASDAQ (COMP:IND) and S&P 500 (SP500) down more than two percent in trading on Tuesday. The Dow (DJI) had its worst daily performance since October. Even with the sharp pullback, the major indexes still trade near all-time highs, and valuations remain stretched viewed via historical metrics.

Neither Greenland nor additional tariffs was on most investors short worry lists to begin 2026. Primary concerns to begin the year were more centered around a potential AI bubble, sticky inflation, and a faltering jobs market, as well as uncertainty around the pace of interest rate cuts from the Federal Reserve in 2026.

One has to wonder what other potential gray swans are potentially out there as we get further into 2026. I consistently try to think about these types of questions. Some of these are nonsensical and have little bearing on the markets. Like wondering why tech titans like Zuckerberg, Bezos, and Thiel, while espousing and investing heavily in the AI Revolution, are building massive bunker complexes.

While engaging more traditional thinking around somewhat "off the radar" issues the markets and the economy could face in 2026, here are some of the potential gray swans on my list.

Japanese Debt:

One of my three predictions for January was the Japanese sovereign debt situation and its impact on the yen-carry trade would get much more airtime to start 2026. This projection has come to fruition as this topic is getting many more mentions in the financial press, such as on CNBC and at the Wall Street Journal.

Japanese debt yields have exploded this week, rising more than 30 bps as new snap elections are called for in the Land of the Rising Sun. The yield on the Japanese 30-Year bond stands around 3.9%. This is the highest level since this maturity began being issued in 1999. The Prime Minister who took office late last year is calling for increased government spending (including doubling defense spending over a five-year period) as well as simultaneous tax cuts by pausing the 8% consumption tax on food purchases.

The nervousness of bond investors is certainly warranted, given Japan's debt-to-GDP ratio is already the highest in the developed world, ending 2025 at just over 250%. Given the country has one of the oldest populations in the world and the economy has experienced anemic growth throughout the 21st century, it is hard to see how Japan solves this dilemma. Could this become a debt contagion for other economies with high debt-to-GDP ratios like France and the U.S.?

Spiking Gold & Silver Prices:

Global investors certainly seem to be signaling they are becoming increasingly concerned about the debt situation in the U.S., Japan, and other slow-growing developed economies across Europe, looking at the huge rallies in both gold and silver over the past year.

In addition to what the spike in gold and silver prices says about global investors and central banks' views on sovereign debt, one has to wonder if a major player could be on the wrong side of these wagers. A large margin call could roil these commodity markets. Rumors of one upset the silver markets in a major way late in December.

Normalization of Interest Rates:

At this point, one also has to wonder if the central bank can ever "normalize" interest rates again without causing a major selloff in the markets. During the Great Financial Crisis, the Federal Reserve took rates down to zero. And for most of the time since a near-zero Fed Funds rate has prevailed in the markets.

And the Federal Reserve has not been able to normalize rates since the GFC without causing a disruption in the markets. There was the infamous taper tantrum of 2013, and worries about a similar occurrence were consistent for most of 2018. The S&P 500 ended down just over four percent that year.

When the Federal Reserve finally did start raising rates aggressively off near zero in March of 2022 in response to a huge spike in inflation, the S&P 500 fell 18% in 2022, and the NASDAQ was off by a third. Higher rates have also hurt the housing sector, as existing home sales have been moribund since the start of 2023. So, a key question for investors should be, given the U.S. debt load, can the Federal Reserve ever normalize rates without tanking the markets?

Especially in light of the rise in the yield of the 30-Year Treasury (US30Y) since the Federal Reserve started cutting rates by a cumulative 175 bps to this point.

What Asset Class Bubble Will Pop Next?

In response to the Covid pandemic, the Federal Reserve boosted the money supply by some 40% in just two years. Congress pumped trillions of more dollars into the economy with Covid stimulus and new infrastructure programs. All of this taxpayer largess triggered the highest level of inflation since the late 1970s/1980s.

This huge surge of dollars pushed nearly every asset class, from stocks and residential real estate to commodities, substantially higher. And it does say something for the resilience of the markets that many asset classes have popped without major impacts to the overall market, to this point.

There was a massive surge of IPOs and SPACs in 2020 and 2021. There was then a huge unwinding of these positions starting in late 2021/early 2022. Many of these SPACs have gone bankrupt. More than a third of SPACs from 2020 as of the spring of 2024, in fact. The overall market just shrugged this off, however.

Office property values have plummeted in most major markets since the pandemic, which resulted in a surge in the virtual workforce and less need for office space. Delinquency rates on Commercial Mortgage-Backed Securities, or CMBS, against office properties have surged to roughly 12%. This is higher than the peak during the GFC. To this point, markets haven't even blinked.

The NFT market—anyone remember them? Has completely tanked. Recently, Bitcoin and other cryptocurrencies have had huge pullbacks that have gotten plenty of attention but haven't yet impacted the equity markets. Will that change in the months ahead?

So, what is the next asset class that will implode, and will this have any impacts on the overall market? One would think the number of asset classes that have come under duress would eventually have a cumulative impact on equities.

And those are some of the key gray swans for markets I am thinking about as I end my day. If you have a potential off-the-radar event you believe could shake the markets, please feel free to post that in the comment section below.

data.title: Cloudflare's Quiet Enterprise Inflection
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Cloudflare, Inc. (NET) is a platform in the process of transitioning from a usage-based platform into a fundamental enterprise infrastructure platform in the areas of security, networking, and AI computation, with its software-defined edge facilitating the consolidation of services and AI inference, supporting its bullish outlook.

From User Growth to Revenue Gravity: Cloudflare’s Enterprise Flywheel Kicks In

Cloudflare ended Q3 with 4,009 large customers contributing over $100,000 per year in annualized revenue, which is up 23% from the prior year. While this alone is great, the bigger picture for me is what’s happening under the hood. These large customers contribute to 73% of overall revenue today compared to 67% from a year ago. It’s not just about collecting logos. It’s about recognizing the clear shift towards maximizing the value from the existing customer base while gradually cutting dependence on smaller customers with higher churn rates.

This, in my opinion, is a significant inflection point in the unit economics. Cloudflare is no longer growing in a way that adds breadth at the low end of the market. Rather, the company is adding more monetization in the enterprise accounts, where the usage compounds. This is significant, as the durability of the growth changes. The revenue is no longer more transactional, but rather more embedded.

This is closely related to the pool-of-funds contract form that management has been intentionally highlighting. These contracts now contribute a low double-digit percentage of the overall contract value per year, says the CFO. The importance lies in the deliberate strategy at play here. Capital is committed up front without being tied to particular products. This eliminates procurement friction and moves spending decisions down to the teams. Consumption follows naturally after that.

The behavioral effect is already reflected in the expansion numbers that Cloudflare is seeing. Customers are using more and more services across its 55+ revenue-generating products, and this is resulting in dollar-based net retention rates rising to 119%, an improvement of five percentage points from the last quarter. I view this as important because it marks the end of a stabilization phase and the return of base-driven expansion. This is not just steady-state, it is expansion in the base, and one of the best kind of expansions that any platform business can possibly experience.

Revenue visibility measures support the above conclusion. The company's remaining performance obligations increased to $2.143 billion, a figure that grew 8% from the prior quarter and a whopping 43% from the same period last year. This outpaces the revenue increase of 30.7%. In my view, the delta indicates that Cloudflare is locking in more contracts of a longer duration and higher value, and not necessarily the short-term, usage-based contracts. In other words, it establishes a pipeline that extends well into fiscal years 2026 and 2028, amid a growing total addressable market.

Sales execution trends also reflect this structural shift. Sales productivity grew for the seventh consecutive quarter, and net sales capacity grew at its fastest pace in more than two years. It is a good sign when both occur together. Adding headcount is not a solution that works very often without improvements in productivity. In this case, both are being accomplished simultaneously. Cloudflare is beginning to reap the rewards of past human capital expenditures as a new set of fully ramped sales reps converts pipeline into booked revenue.

Crucially, the architecture of the Cloudflare platform enables this enterprise success to drive operating leverage rather than increased capital expense. Gross margin remained flat at 75.3%, right in the target range of 75% to 77%. This is because Cloudflare’s homogenous global network means every server can support every service. Large enterprise business doesn’t need customized infrastructure, even at scale. This leverage can be understood clearly by looking at some of the deals that have happened recently.

Deals like the $22.8 million deal with a Global 2000 media portal or the $34.3 million deal with a European technology company were handled by utilizing existing capacities. This is what turns top-line growth into operating leverage. Demand driven by AI-related technologies adds another incremental catalyst to this business dynamic. Though a minor contributor to revenues the management indicated that about 80% of the top AI firms are actually using Cloudflare. This usually begins with security before moving into inference work. The way I view AI as a revenue driver would be less about a pure driver in itself but rather a strong entry point to draw customers in.

When Valuation Leaves No Room to Breathe

Cloudflare has a capital structure and valuation profile that operate at a severe disconnect from its GAAP profitability. The valuation multiples indicate that the market prices Cloudflare stock at perfection. It is leaving no margin for operational error and macro deceleration. This disparity between the NET stock price and its financial performance is that depends entirely on stabilizing hyper-growth rates instead of generating statutory earnings. NET’s P/E non-GAAP ratio stands at 202x on a forward basis against a sector median of 25.9x. This implies a premium of 678%.

Further, the forward EV/Sales ratio is 29.8x roughly ~744% higher than the sector median of 3.53x. These multiples mean that the Street expects Cloudflare to grow into this valuation over an extended period. But the PEG non-GAAP (forward) ratio of 7.52x is 354% above the sector median. This means even when factoring in the growth rate, the NET stock price remains expensive and sensitive. For instance, following the Human Native acquisition announcement, the NET stock price fell more than 2%.

Furthermore, Bank of America noted that an outage affecting major platforms like X and ChatGPT could cause reputational risk. For Cloudflare’s stock priced at ~30x sales, any reputational damage may cause a deceleration in client acquisition, and an increase in churn may catalyze a rapid multiple compression.

Additionally, gross margin compression is a risk to the sentiment required to support these multiples. Q3 gross margin declining to 75.3% from 76.3% YoY, and management linked this to a higher mix of paid traffic and product mix shifts. Still within the long-term target, a continued downward trend in TTM gross margin % can erode the argument that Cloudflare warrants a software-like multiple, and with that, the justification for a 201x P/E multiple evaporates.

Overall, Cloudflare needs to stabilize topline growth above 30% to maintain its current market cap. The Rule of 40 is hit primarily through the revenue component and not the profit component. If topline growth decelerates to the mid-20% range, the support for the current enterprise value will disintegrate, as there are no GAAP earnings to provide a valuation floor. This necessitates flawless execution when hyperscalers/enterprises are trying to optimize for AI and security budgets. Therefore, the lack of GAAP profitability with extreme valuation multiples forms a structural weakness that boosts downside risk.

Takeaway

Cloudflare is moving from growth based on usage to contract-driven, enterprise-scale. The 43% RPO is a strong indicator of locked-in demand well before the revenue numbers, in addition to increasing penetration of full-paying clients, which provides visibility for the next several years. If they stay the course, the current revenue trajectory will feed into the future cycle.

data.title: Tootsie Roll Industries: Growth Isn't Enough To Justify Optimism
data.description:
I know that anecdotes don't really mean much in the grand scheme of things. Having said that, from my observations over my 36 years of life, people either love Tootsie Rolls or they hate them. I'm in the former category. But I can appreciate that they aren't for everyone. The company at the center of these delicious morsels, is none other than Tootsie Roll Industries, Inc. (TR), a rather small business in the confectionary market.

At this moment, the company has a market capitalization of only $2.62 billion. But it has a strong balance sheet and has been growing steadily as of late. Unfortunately, even though the picture for the business has been showing signs of improvement, the stock is not really all that appealing from a valuation standpoint. And that is why, back in June of last year, I reaffirmed the company as a Hold candidate. I wish I could turn bullish on it right now. But as things stand, I think that would be premature.

Getting a little tastier

From a purely fundamental standpoint, I would argue that Tootsie Roll Industries is doing reasonably well right now. Take the third quarter of the 2025 fiscal year as an example. During that quarter, revenue for the company amounted to $232.7 million. That's an increase over the $225.9 million the business reported a year earlier. This increase was driven by a rise in product sales from $223.9 million to $230.6 million. Based on the data provided, this increase can be chalked up to domestic sales growth of 3.8%. Unfortunately, we did not see any sales growth overseas. In fact, foreign revenue dropped 6.1% year over year.

As great as the domestic sales growth was to see, however, it is worth noting that the rise here was driven at least in part by the timing of sales, including those involving seasonal activities, that essentially boosted revenue more in the third quarter of 2025 than it otherwise would have been.

In my previous article about the firm, I detailed how the price of cocoa had skyrocketed since 2023. Hence since the start of 2024, it has gone up absurdly. To put this in perspective, back in December of 2022, the price per metric ton of cocoa was $2,456.20. That number as of June of last year, which is the most recent month for which we have data available, stood at $8,401.95 per ton. That is an increase of 3.42 times. There are multiple drivers behind this. One source, Dr. Tonya Lander who is amongst other things a researcher at the Oxford Martin School Programme on the Future of Food, detailed how, really starting in the first half of 2024, the price of Coco roughly quadrupled.

Some of this can be attributed to the ongoing effects of climate change. They affect the $100 billion chocolate industry in meaningful ways. One contributor to this is El Nino, which occurs every two to seven years and that involves warmer than average temperatures in the Pacific Ocean creating volatile weather patterns. Unfortunately, cocoa trees and their pollinators are sensitive to these weather fluctuations, namely changes in weather and the availability of water. Unfortunately, El Nino weather has a history of impacting cocoa harvests in Cote d’Ivoire and Ghana, which is where between 60% and 70% of all cocoa beans in the world are produced.

Climate change has exacerbated this, making weather even more volatile than it otherwise would be. But you also have other factors as well. Tariffs are problematic when it comes to the U.S. because literally all Coco is imported. But you have more than just the direct impact on chocolate prices alone. Higher quality imported chocolate from the European Union is also impacted because of a 20% tariff. And when you add on top of this significant tariffs on Chinese imports, such as those involving packaging materials, you have compounding effects here. Add in to the mix the effect of other events such as the growth of the gold mining industry and disease, and it's not shocking that prices would rise.

Such a huge increase in pricing unfortunately is problematic for an American consumer that is already struggling. Management specifically mentioned that customers and consumers have become more resistant to higher prices. As the chart above illustrates, consumer confidence levels are near all time lows. So it is shocking to me that revenue increased whatsoever here at home. Having said that, the American consumer is more resilient than international consumers generally are. And that helps to explain why price increases have more severely impacted the international market and pushed sales there down.

Despite these problems, management has done a fine job when it comes to profits and cash flows. Net income grew from $32.8 million in the third quarter of 2024 to $35.7 million in the third quarter of 2025. Operating cash flow inched up from $61.3 million to $62.2 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, however, we do get a drop from $37.5 million to $34.8 million. Meanwhile, EBITDA for the company remained flat at $44.5 million.

In the chart above, you can see financial results for the first nine months of 2025 compared to the same time of 2024. Revenue, profits, and most cash flow metrics, all improved during this time. And on top of this, the company has succeeded in continuing to grow its book value per share. This is encouraging in and of itself, with the metric growing to $12.61 in the third quarter of last year compared to the $12.20 per share reported at the end of 2024. Regardless, I like seeing this positive trend.

It's also important to note that while the balance sheet of the company has gotten a bit weaker, on the whole, the picture looks solid. In the chart below, you can see precisely what I mean. The firm actually has cash in excess of debt amounting to $111.7 million. That is unfortunately down from the $187.5 million that it ended 2024 with. But it's still a testament to the overall health of the company and signals that the risk of a solvency or liquidity issue is incredibly small absent a severe and long term downturn.

All of these are net positives to me. But where I struggle is the valuation of the business. If we project out results based on what we saw during the first nine months of 2025, we would get net profits of $96.2 million, adjusted operating cash flow of $117.2 million, and EBITDA totaling $144.4 million. These would all be slightly better than what the company saw in 2024. But still, the stock is pricey.

In the chart below, you can see exactly how expensive shares are. In addition to being costly on an absolute basis, the stock is also near the higher end of the spectrum compared to other similar firms. In that same chart, I stacked it up against five such enterprises. On a price to earnings basis, only two of these companies ended up being cheaper than it is. But four were cheaper when it came to the EV to EBITDA approach. And when it came to the price to operating cash flow approach, Tootsie Roll Industries ended up being the most expensive of the group.

The picture is more moderate when we use the price to book multiple. I already detailed earlier in the article how the book value per share of the business has been on the rise. And that has certainly helped its valuation. In the chart below, you can see that the firm is trading at a price to book multiple of 2.91. That is still higher than three of the five institutions that I compared it to in that chart. But that still means that it puts the company around the middle of the group.

Takeaway

What I see here at this moment is a company that is doing remarkably well for the moment. Obviously, I am concerned about the industry more broadly. But overall revenue, profits, and cash flows, are all expanding. The company has a solid balance sheet, and it is trading at levels that look to be in the fair value range compared to other comparable firms. Given this, I believe that TR justifies more of a Hold rating them it does anything else.

data.title: lululemon: Beware Of Turnaround Investing In Retail
data.description:
lululemon athletica (LULU) stock is down an astounding 60% from their 2023 peak as sales growth decelerated sharply, gross margins dropped, and operating margins narrowed.

While consensus estimates remain subdued, expecting continued struggles for the athletic wear company, the stock is showing some signs of life, up nearly 18% from the September 2025 bottom.

Amid a looming proxy fight, a better-than-expected holiday quarter, intensifying competition, and a seemingly attractive valuation, turnaround investors are left conflicted.

To me, in retail, the best move is to wait on the sidelines until actual change occurs. The cost of catching a falling knife is far greater than the prospect of missing out on the initial bounce.

The Curse of Popularity in Fashion

When investors assess a LULU investment today, they look at the company through a much different lens compared to a few years ago.

From the late 2010s until 2023, lululemon took the world by storm, with high-quality athleisure style that became widely popular. Sales have skyrocketed, margins were high, and the stock surged. Back then, it was right to value the company as a fast-growing business, but not anymore.

Whenever you achieve success in fashion retail, that's exactly when you should start getting worried, as no trend goes unnoticed by competitors. Very quickly, many players jumped aboard the athleisure train. Names like Alo Yoga and Vuori took lululemon head-on, and other brands developed their dedicated collections.

Concurrently, customers who were previously willing to pay top dollar for lululemon's quality have started feeling financially pressured and have started trading down.

As a result, instead of being the hottest name in retail, lululemon turned into a brand in need of a turnaround, and that's how it should be evaluated for investment today.

With shares in a 60% drawdown, the stock is surfacing on the radar of turnaround investors.

The Metrics to Monitor for Potential Turnaround

For retail companies, everything comes down to three metrics—comparable sales, gross margins, and operating margins. No business weakness could avoid the “net” of this combination, and no success can go unnoticed.

On the comparable sales front, Lulu has clearly been deteriorating, with China the lone bright spot:

This essentially shows that with the same footprint, lululemon is selling less, which means lower traffic in stores, or lower average tickets, or both. For LULU, Americas, the largest region, has been flat or down since Q1-24, with no sign of improvement.

Until comparable sales show some sign of life and finding a bottom, the risk for further deterioration remains high.

Then, there's gross margin. This is a clear tell of whether Lulu needs to do discounts and promotions to drive sales, and this is where pricing power is being reflected.

If gross margin is dropping, it means that at the very least, lululemon is unable to pass higher costs to customers, which means it's operating on its back foot. As we can see, gross margins reached a three-year low last quarter, at 55.5%.

So, again, not only are comparable sales pressured, they're of lower quality, as they are being achieved with worse margins.

Lastly, there's operating margin. This is where declining comparable sales really hurt, because you operate the same number of stores (i.e., paying roughly the same number of employees and the same amount of rent) but generate fewer sales from them.

This is the opposite side of operational leverage.

Further, when brands struggle, they could decide to increase marketing spend in hopes of reigniting desirability. When the return on marketing spend worsens, it immediately shows.

Here, as well, we're seeing that Lulu has dropped to multi-year lows.

So, it's undeniable that lululemon is a story of deteriorating fundamentals, with the company going through a rough stretch. And, as long as this keeps going, I find it highly risky to jump on the stock.

Why are investors so eager to invest here? The answer is valuation.

Valuation at Distressed Levels

There's a decently large group of investors who are drawn to turnaround stories. This group is waiting for the next big name that falls off and goes through some crisis so that they can buy in at the lows, betting on an eventual “return to glory.”

Unlike many other names I've covered, including Starbucks (SBUX), Kering (PPRUF), and others, I argue that lululemon does in fact meet the criteria of a potential turnaround investment.

At $202 per share, lululemon trades at 15 times low-cycle earnings, quite a low-level relative to other popular retail brands.

In the event of a turnaround, I think it wouldn't be bold to assume lululemon could fetch a low-twenties multiple, similar to peers like Ralph Lauren (RL) or Tapestry (TPR), and still be far below its own historical levels at peak.

If we assume lululemon returns to its FY24 peak EPS level of $14.67 and apply a 20x multiple, we get to a $293 price target in a cautious turnaround scenario, reflecting nearly 50% upside.

Meanwhile, if no turnaround occurs, I argue lululemon could fall to as low as a 10x P/E, which would mean a 35% decline. So, we can already see that there's an attractive risk-reward setup here.

That said, I still wouldn't want to buy lululemon here.

Beware of Turnaround Investing in Retail

Peter Lynch popularized turnaround investing as a way for individual investors to generate alpha. In turnarounds, there's always a tension between wanting to fully capitalize on a recovery (which means entering the position before everybody else jumps on the bandwagon) and wanting to avoid catching a falling knife (which means waiting until tangible signs of change).

Personally, I would much rather miss some upside than buy too early.

Put yourself in the shoes of a Nike (NKE) investor who has been buying the stock over the past few years:

Or, in the shoes of an Abercrombie & Fitch investor, a company that successfully turned things around, but it took more than a decade:

In fashion retail, unlike other sectors, a turnaround is typically not about operational efficiencies, trimming unnecessary fat, changing the go-to-market strategy, or innovating on the business model front.

In retail, a turnaround mainly comes down to, somehow, having the right design idea fall in your lap, just at the right time, and catch fire on social media. That is, at least in my view, a highly risky and unpredictable event to chase as investors.

Conclusion

lululemon is still in a down-cycle, contrary to what the recent 26% relief rally might suggest. Comparable sales in key regions are still negative or stagnant, and margins are still contracting.

While valuation does skew toward an attractive risk-reward set-up, I don't find it attractive enough to justify buying here.

Retail turnarounds are extremely tough and unpredictable, and I believe the right decision would be to wait on the sidelines, even if it means giving up on the initial upside.

As such, I rate lululemon a “hold.”

data.title: UPS Is Out Of The Hole, And My Portfolio (Downgrade)
data.description:
I've been covering United Parcel Service (UPS) for a while now, and for most of my coverage, I gave it a "Hold" rating and was fairly disparaging about the company's prospects, even after the recent executive changes and parting ways with Amazon (AMZN).

Then, in September of last year, I gave the "Buy" signal with an article titled "Calling the UPS Bottom," which is now one of my most viewed articles on Seeking Alpha, out of the over 500 that I've written.

Here's an illustration of my rating history, but you can see all of it here:

This rise surprised me and enriched me, since I did actually buy UPS shares shortly after that article was published. I didn't expect the market to be so enthusiastic about UPS's prospects so quickly.

UPS Still Has a Growth Problem

The ever-changing trade policy coming down from Washington (currently in the Supreme Court), the end of the de minimis rule, and ongoing slowdowns in overall consumption in the economy have not made UPS any more profitable than the last time I covered it, and that's something that I thought would need to change before the market would readjust its view.

That's why I'm surprised. I thought I had way more time after my "Buy" signal before we'd see the rise we have, up 27% since then.

Net income last quarter was up, but not enough to demonstrate to me that anything is really changing.

In Q3, one of the big headlines was revenue per package increasing, which is a positive and did contribute to the rise in net income. But it's a small rise, and overall revenues haven't materially changed.

In September, UPS was a much cheaper stock than it is today, based on its forward PE ratio, although 15 still isn't shabby. Earnings did grow some during that time, too, so it's not all the price increase at work.

That's not too expensive for me, and I'll discuss later why UPS is leaving my portfolio, even if I'd still consider it not expensive.

Meanwhile, gross profit margins compressed last quarter, a bad sign, and one that UPS was not punished for, thankfully. It was a hiccup, but not a major one.

At least operating margin is up, which is one of the more closely watched metrics for UPS and one that I predicted would increase following the cutting loose of Amazon, a reason I gave for my upgrade in September.

UPS Earnings Next Week

The next earnings event for UPS is on Jan. 27, and I expect it to be a volatile catalyst, so make sure you are positioned properly going into that day. UPS earnings will be before the market opens.

Metric Estimate
Revenue $24B
Operating Margin 11%
EPS $2.20

I expect operating margin to be a potential sore spot. If it doesn't continue to improve and stays flat, UPS could be punished. The stock will definitely be punished if the report comes in negative on that metric.

Last quarter, domestic revenue declined alongside volume, but the revenue per piece improved, which was a big needle mover for the market. This increase can also be attributed to the end of the de minimis rule, where UPS is no longer carting around a lot of low-value items.

While UPS is still in the midst of restructuring itself, and I expect this to be covered in the earnings call, what remains to be seen is if driver wages, famously unionized, will eat through the margins created by the restructuring and per-package revenue increases.

In this last year, they've been successful at reducing their headcount while increasing revenue per employee. That figure still isn't above the 2023 peak, which was also the peak of their total employees.

Of course, the last thing to watch out for in the earnings call will be the 2026 outlook. I expect them to be rosy about it as they're coming out of the hole still, and it seems that they're doing it. Especially if they're able to report positive operating margin growth, then I expect them to have a rosy outlook for 2026. If their outlook is negative, I expect a big drop, potentially 10% or more.

Current options pricing is implying a 7% move during the earnings event, so even traders are bracing for some volatility here.

Why I'm Selling My UPS Position

I expect UPS to have a poor earnings call, and I don't want to continue holding onto the business. When I entered this position, it was never as a long-term holding, and I noted before I got in, in the last article in September, that position sizing mattered a lot here. I think that's still true, and I caution investors who are not exiting before the earnings call to make sure that they have either healthy long-term conviction or safety in their position sizing.

There are macro headwinds that make me think UPS did not get enough volume during Q4 to meet their revenue targets.

It makes sense that their volume would keep declining, especially since the volume of imports from China is falling. This makes me hesitant to think that UPS would be able to do better now than they did in Q3.

The same goes for the EU, which has seen imports fall off a cliff since Liberation Day and the 15% tariff placed on them. No pick-up in sight, bad for UPS. A bright spot is that exports picked up, and UPS is relying on international shipping to fill in the gaps for missing Amazon revenue.

Now with extra tariffs because of the Greenland issue, I can't expect a very good Q1 for the following earnings either. These trends will not reverse soon.

Beyond this, there's an argument to be made about the consumer pulling back. While more money was spent this year on holiday shopping, and that would largely include packages that were shipped, the places that people spent changed. We saw a rise in visits to discount and dollar stores and a year-over-year fall in superstore traffic this year.

This also included a rise in visits to off-price retailers and thrift stores, which would have also cut down on the amount of packages they get shipped. The more these physical stores are getting traffic, the less traffic is being sent through online shopping and shipped products from larger retailers using UPS.

Combine all of this with having to go into an earnings event, which I feel negatively about. Now up 27% in such a short time frame from September to January, and it just feels like it's the right time to close this speculative bet.

Long-term UPS shareholders are likely to be unaffected by any of these changes, as the ultimate decider of UPS's trajectory will be its ability to reshape itself into a much more efficient machine. I was willing to take that bet thinking that I would have longer to accumulate a position, but it's moved out of my comfort zone very quickly.

With the current macro headwinds I see in front of UPS, I just don't want to take the risk of losing from here. And I think that there's significant room to fall and still be in line with the long-term thesis about restructuring.

So, income investors holding UPS for the dividend and any long-term growth they're able to squeeze out, although lately that's been fairly little, may not need to heed my warning. But short-term investors who were trading UPS and followed me in when I called the bottom should consider trimming their positions before earnings or exiting as I intend to do in the coming days.

It does have a 6% yield, after all.

Conclusion

Ultimately, I think that UPS still has a decent long-term trajectory, and that its prospects of being successful in its reorganization are still likely to be successful. However, it's unclear to me how much more UPS will be rewarded by the market for this success, and that much of the narrative of a UPS turnaround is now priced in.

Because I see macro headwinds ahead for domestic shipping and international shipping, I am less bullish about UPS and am not willing to sit through the upcoming earnings report. For me, this trade is over, and I'm returning UPS to its previous "Hold" rating.

Those who are going to hold until the earnings report should be watching for operating margins, overall volume, and revenue per package. I believe operating margins need to increase for the stock to react positively.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: Artisan Mid Cap Value Fund Q4 2025 Performance Discussion And Portfolio Activity
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Artisan Mid Cap Value Fund Q4 2025 Commentary.

The portfolio slightly outperformed the Russell Midcap® Value Index in Q4. As we have noted in prior quarterly portfolio letters, our investment style has been out-of-step with the equity markets for the better part of the last two years as growth and momentum factors led, with readings on certain measures that track these factors nearing historical extremes. The portfolio’s relative performance notably improved when the market rotation took root in November. After falling behind the index in October, the portfolio gained ground in November and December. For the quarter, sector positioning contributed favorably due to above-benchmark weightings in health care and information technology and below-benchmark weightings in energy and real estate. Our consumer discretionary, energy and financials holdings were sources of positive stock selection, offset by underperformance among our technology and communication services holdings.

Online travel agency Expedia (EXPE), our top overall contributor in Q4, drove performance in the consumer discretionary sector. Travel and leisure spending globally has secular tailwinds, and Expedia has continued to execute well. Total bookings grew 12% year over year in the company’s latest quarter, with strength across its Expedia, Hotels.com and Vrbo brands. Growth has been supported by supply expansion and product enhancements, with AI powering features such as property Q&A, guest review summaries and service agents. Since the COVID-driven downturn of 2020, the company has delivered strong free cash flow, while remaining disciplined in its capital allocation. In addition to a small dividend that was reinstated in 2025 after suspension during the pandemic, the company has repurchased shares at a remarkable rate, having an average annualized net buyback yield of 7.7% over the past three years.

Our top performers in the energy and financials sectors were NOV (NOV), a provider of oilfield equipment, technology and expertise, and First Citizens (FCNCA), a North Carolina-based bank. NOV’s quarterly earnings exceeded expectations on the back of strong execution and margin improvement in its energy equipment segment, which more than offset softer conditions in shorter cycle businesses. Free cash flow generation was particularly strong, reflecting both higher profitability and ongoing working capital efficiency gains. Capital allocation remains shareholder-friendly, with NOV continuing to return excess cash through dividends and share repurchases while maintaining a conservative balance sheet, with low net leverage and ample liquidity. From a valuation perspective, its shares trade at a discount to historical and peer EV/EBITDA multiples, supported by a high free cash flow yield and improving cash flow consistency.

First Citizens had been a big winner following its 2023 acquisition of the failed Silicon Valley Bank (SVB); however, shares had trailed peers for much of 2025 due to asset sensitivity concerns, credit quality issues and a falling return on tangible common equity (ROTCE). These issues seem overblown. Asset sensitivity refers to assets (loans and other earning securities) repricing faster than liabilities (borrowings). First Citizens should soon be able to replace its 3.5% fixed-rated note from the FDIC issued to finance the SVB purchase with low-cost variable rate funding or deposits, reducing asset sensitivity. With regard to credit quality, First Citizens had exposure to the bankruptcy of First Brands, the US auto parts maker, though we believe First Brands is unlikely to represent a broader issue. Lastly, ROTCE has been temporarily depressed due to higher costs associated with regulatory capital requirements under Basel III. The stock has been moving higher since October, when it reported better-than-expected earnings driven by an acceleration in SVB-related activity. With shares trading at a significant discount to peers, a robust stock buyback program and the potential for improved ROTCE and loan growth, we remain investors.

Portfolio returns in the technology and communication services sectors were hurt by Vontier (VNT), CDW (CDW) and Pinterest (PINS). Vontier is a provider of fuel dispensing and related software and services to retail and commercial gas stations. Shares reacted negatively after its late-October earnings release despite beating modestly on earnings and revenue. The company reported a slight sales increase and better-than-expected earnings in its Q3, but core sales were essentially flat year over year as strength in mobility technologies was offset by weakness in repair solutions. Investors may have also been disappointed with its muted outlook for 2026 sales growth. Though the stock has been flat over the past year, Vontier has been one of our top contributors since we purchased it in 2022 when it was under pressure due to a mix of headwinds, including a payment technology upgrade cycle that had pulled forward sales, supply chain disruptions, higher gas prices in the early months of the Ukraine war and longer term fears regarding electric vehicle market share growth. While growth has slowed, the business has continued to generate strong free cash flow generation and deliver high returns on capital.

CDW is a value-added reseller (VAR) of information technology solutions that we added to the portfolio in April when IT resellers pulled back alongside the broader sector. While revenue continues to grow at a mid-single-digit rate, investors remain focused on the pace of operating leverage, and little has changed fundamentally since our initial purchase. Concerns about disintermediation—particularly as cloud computing expands—have long accompanied the VAR model and are not new. Similar worries emerged during the industry’s shift from physical to digital software delivery and later with the rise of software-as-a-service. Today, the perceived risk centers on cloud vendors selling directly to customers. Disintermediation risk is more pronounced in the enterprise market, where customers have the scale and expertise to manage vendor relationships internally. Small and mid-sized businesses (SMBs), by contrast, generally lack the resources to evaluate, integrate and support an increasingly complex universe of IT products and therefore continue to rely on VARs for selection, implementation and ongoing support. Vendors also generally have limited incentive to build out direct sales and service capabilities for SMBs. CDW benefits from attractive business economics, with low capital intensity and strong free cash flow generation. While IT spending is cyclical, CDW has historically grown faster than overall IT spend as the channel has continued to gain share from direct sales over time.
